Morning.
Morning.

10 minutes.

Morning. Morning, Mr. Chairman and members of the committee.
I'm Catherine Malloy, the Chief Executive of Crayate Rock Lobster Industry
Association. I've brought along Mark Edwards, who you'll know,
for any spicy questions.
We represent the Crayate fishery in Southern New Zealand.
Crayate is arguably the most abundant lobster fishery of anywhere in
the world,
but it wasn't always that way.
So if we start with one fishing pot, let's say it's yours, Mr. Chairman.
In 1992, if you pulled your pot from the waters of
Crayate, you would end up with less than one cray fish for dinner.
Our fishery was in critical condition.
Today, you'd pull up over 16 legal sized crays, which is
much more than a bag limit. And on a good day, my fishermen
tell me you'd often get over 150 cray fish in a pull.
This is what we mean by abundance. In Southland we say, if you're a
fisher or a diver and you can't catch a cray in Crayate-
... you should probably stick to fishing at Pattersons.
This didn't happen because the government mandated it.
Our organization, supported by our quota owners,
developed a rebuild management procedure that reduced the
TACC from 1,150 to
570 tons. We funded the science, we contributed the
data, and we held the line to rebuild the fishery to the
1,392 tons it is today.
The rebuild happened because of the tools we're asking you to formalize in
this bill.
The tools aren't about taking more, they're about managing smarter
and faster to ensure the future of our fisheries.
Crayate contributes $190 million in export earnings per
year. Yes.
But more importantly, we're the 65 fishing crews.
We're the young fishermen trying to build a life like their tupuna before them,
and we're the small regional communities like Rakiura Stewart Island,
where fishing isn't a lifestyle, it's a living.
There are four essential parts of this bill for us.
The most important is the recognition of management procedures.
Right now, without a management procedure, the TACC is
set and can be left unchanged for what can be five years to a
decade, regardless of what's happening in the water.
In a world of marine heat waves and mortality events,
a static TACC is a huge risk,
and that uncertainty has very real cost.
Picture a young fisherman sitting across from a bank manager and asking for a loan
of $3 million to buy a vessel. The bank manager leans
forward.
"What guarantee have you got that the fish will even be there in the future?"
It's a question he can't answer.
We're losing the next generation, not because they don't want to fish, but because
the system gives them nothing solid to build a business on.
The management procedures fix that.
It's a publicly consulted rule that adjusts the TACC each
year based on the most recent data.
It's not just CPUE, it's biomass, it's sex
ratios, and it's migration patterns.
If the biomass increases, then so does the TACC.
If it falls below a threshold,
the response is automatic and it's swift.
It's disciplined, transparent, and science-based management.
Crayate is the proof.
We've been running management procedures for 25 years, and they work.
Without the amendments, management procedures have no formal legislative
home, and without that, there's no guarantee we'll even be allowed
one in our next stock assessment.
We simply cannot run New Zealand's best fishery blind.
The second tool we need is a formal recognition of industry voluntary
measures, particularly catch shelving.
Catch shelving is where quota owners voluntarily set aside
catch to protect the long-term,
um,
health of their stocks. That kind of discipline deserves a formal place
in law. If you want a proactive industry that supports
abundant fisheries, you must give us the tools to enable it.
The third is tiered stock management.
The current framework requires the same process for every stock, regardless
of how much data exists. Not every fishery can be
Crayate. We measured 85,000 fish last
year.
Others can't do that.
Crayate will meet the highest data requirements and-
but fisheries with less should still get proactive management
rather than being thrown in the too hard basket.
Finally, carry forward, enabling unused catch to be carried into the following
season in a limited capacity with the majority of
quota ownership and agreement.
It's not about gaming the system. It's about flexibility when
disruption is beyond our control.
When COVID-19 closed China's live seafood market overnight,
our fishers couldn't land catch that was swimming in abundance right below
them.
The fish was there, the quota was there, the market was not.
Quota owners should not permanently lose their catch because of an event they had
no, um,
part in causing.
So our ask is clearEnsure management procedures,
recognition of voluntary measures and tiered stock assessments are
included. These are not untested theories, they're proven tools that are
internationally recognized as best practice, and they're
essential in regions of New Zealand where fishing is important
and it's not something that people only do if they can't golf.
We went from barely one crayfish per pot to over 16, and we did it with
management procedures, voluntary restraint and a commitment to doing the
right thing.
This bill backs the tools that are already working and gives them a place in
law, and we urge the committee to support the bill.
Thank you.
Thank you. Rachel has a question.
Thank you, Chair. Thank you Catherine, for your submission.
Got a question about the management procedures.
Mm-hmm.
I guess given that they are voluntary and can already be instigated-
Yeah
... what's the benefit of actually having them in law, given that you can do it

process around management procedures, and it gives us a way of
being able to bring multiple voices to the table when we're designing the
management procedures. The second one is we've seen a lot
of challenge to the use of management procedures, and we're already hearing for our
next stock assessment that we're unlikely to get a management procedure.
So for us, if the management procedures aren't formalized, there's
a real risk that we're only going to have
that stock assessment and then a wait until the next stock assessment.
Just to sup on that.
Yeah.

essentially the legislation would allow
overriding of the industry if the management procedure needed to be put in place.
Is that what it is?

Yeah.
Okay.

net, and then we step up based on increases in
the biomass above that. If our management procedure

ability to increase the stock

it gives us a way of designing what we think
the risk is. So for Cray 8,

within that quota,

into how many fish they think is in the full area, and then

And that's
just for crayfish, it's not the entire biomass.

Yeah.

Oh, one other thing. You
raised this thing about the interruption with COVID-
... and also it happened with,
obviously there was significant issues as a result of Gabrielle.
Yeah.
So
in your mind, is the bill sufficient to manage
those-

In certain circumstances

We want it to be for things that are out of the fishery's control that don't have
an impact on sustainability.
Perfect. Thank you very much.

Thanks.

Kia ora koutou. Ko Emily Hall toku ingoa.
I'm a campaign manager at SAFE, and we thank you for the
opportunity to speak today.
SAFE is Aotearoa, New Zealand's leading animal rights organization,
representing tens of thousands of Kiwis who value
compassion and expect animal welfare laws to be properly
enforced.
We oppose the Fisheries Amendment Bill and urge the committee to reject
it. Our main concern is that this bill prioritizes commercial and
industry interests over transparency, the welfare of
animals, environmental protections, and upholding legal
obligations.
My first point relates to animal sentience being ignored.
Fishes are legally recognized as sentient beings under the Animal
Welfare Act 1999, meaning they have the capacity to
experience pain,
fear,
distress, and a sense of wellbeing.
However, this bill creates a clear inconsistency between the
Fisheries Act and the Animal Welfare Act,
treats fishes as commodities rather than animals, does not require
fisheries decision-makers to consider fish welfare,
permits commercial fishing practices that cause prolonged suffering,
and undermines the New Zealand government's own legal recognition of
animal sentience.
My second point relates to the bill expanding management flexibility
without introducing welfare safeguards,
and as a result, practices causing severe and preventable suffering would
continue unchecked. This includes prolonged
asphyxiation during capture and landing,
crushing and barotrauma from rapid depth changes, and
handling, gutting, and processing without stunning.
Despite decades of evidence of harm to animals, fisheries
legislation does not mandate welfare standards for capture,
handling, stunning, or slaughter, and these practices are not meaningfully
addressed within fisheries management frameworks.
We submit that this constitutes harm by omission.
I'd like to briefly touch on bycatch, an issue in New Zealand that exposes
major regulatory failure. Non-target fishes are
injured during capture, exposed due to ear and pressure
trauma, and discarded dead or dying,
often without being recorded. In terms of
bycatch, this bill focuses on operational certainty rather than
recognizing bycatch injury and death as an
animal welfare failure.
Whilst fish farming is not primarily governed by the Fisheries Act,
this act does cover some aspects relating to this industry, and I'd like to
speak to this for a moment, because intensive fish farming is
simply underwater factory farming.
There are significant welfare issues associated with fish
farming, but this bill frames those issues as economic or
environmental issues rather than animal welfare issues.
Large-scale mortality events often occur without investigation,
any follow-up welfare assessment, or consequences for those
responsible, which, quite frankly, would be unheard of in any other farming
sector.
In terms of transparency and accountability, this bill
reduces public scrutiny, restricts access to onboard camera
footage, limits transparency around animal welfare outcomes,
and leans on voluntary industry-led measures over enforceable
standards, which in turn undermines public trust,
consumer confidence, and perhaps most importantly, New
Zealand's international reputation on animal welfare.
The bottom line here is that transparency must be central, not
optional.
We are also seeing these same issues reflected in parallel policy
settings, including the fast-tracking of large-scale fish farming
projects. That process is characterized by limited
participation, reduced transparency, and exclusion of fish
welfare expertise, despite clear and well-documented
welfare risks.
Fish farming developments can be advanced through a framework that effectively
sidelines animal welfare considerations at the point of
decision-making. Taken together with this bill, that signals a
broader policy direction that prioritizes industry over the
welfare of animals, environmental protections, and
democratic public scrutiny.
The International Aquatic Animal Alliance, of which SAFE is a member,
provided a submission on behalf of 174
organizations, warning from an incredibly well-informed,
expert international perspective that this bill will
weaken marine protections, reduce transparency, increase
reliance on industry self-reporting, expand
discarding allowances, and further marginalize
aquatic animal welfare.
The submission from this international alliance once again called for the rejection
of this bill or, at minimum, substantial amendment.
My final point relates to precedence and a perceived abuse of
democratic process. Sadly, the process undertaken for this bill has
mirrored repeated use by the coalition government of urgency-style
processes for non-urgent legislation and therefore
risks damaging the democratic process.
While not formally under urgency, the rushed consultation
timeframe undermines meaningful public engagement and
appears to abuse parliamentary urgency.This also raises
broader questions about process.
Under the current coalition government, we have seen various pieces of legislation
that impact the animals and environmental protections
progressed at pace despite substantial public opposition.
When that level of public input is not meaningfully reflected, it raises
serious questions around transparency and
accountability, leading to a significant decrease in
public confidence in the system and our democratic processes.
In conclusion, we would like to convey that this bill represents a significant
regression in fisheries governance.
SAFE urges the Primary Production Committee to reject the Fisheries Amendment
Bill in its entirety,
to ensure fisheries law aligns with the Animal Welfare Act, to
explicitly recognize fish sentience and fisheries management, and
commit to a future framework that reduces and ultimately
eliminates practices that result in the
suffering of fishes.
Fishes are legally recognized as sentient beings, and it is high time our
fisheries legislation reflected that reality.
Kia ora, thank you, and I welcome any questions.
